title: Tidebound Promises
summary: I am Mira Solace, a municipal resilience scientist rooted in the Old Pier Neighborhood. In a rising-arc story of steady progress, I navigate technical plans, community grief, and a rekindled romance with Arin Voss as Solace Harbor must choose how to survive and who will survive with it. The pressure point is Aegis Corporation’s proposal — fast, engineered floodwalls and relocations — championed by Liora Kade. My work and my heart are on the line: I convene assemblies in the Tidehouse, sketch tidal gradients into my worn leather notebook, and argue for community-led, ecologically integrated adaptations with craft and evidence. Arin galvanizes grassroots energy. Samir mediates technical nuance; Etta grounds us in memory; Noah anchors the personal stakes. Branches let me pursue scaling community-led solutions, negotiating hybrid policy wins, or steering municipal processes to enable equitable relocation with restitution. Every branch advances upward: relationships deepen or transform into mature partnerships, community capacity grows, and Solace Harbor gains resilience in different forms.

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: I begin the day at the Tidehouse, checking tide logs and meeting community members; I feel the first ripple of momentum as small experiments show promising data.
I host a community assembly in the Old Pier Neighborhood to present data and listen; trust grows as neighbors share repair strategies and elders recount place-knowledge.

Liora unveils Aegis’s fast floodwall plan at a public briefing. I must choose how to respond: directly oppose, propose a hybrid negotiation, or partner with Arin for a public campaign. Each choice charts a different kind of progress.

major_decision_point.description: Whether to push for grassroots alternatives, negotiate a hybrid plan through municipal channels, or partner with Arin for a public campaign.

options.label: Push grassroots alternatives
options.consequence: Deepens community capacity and hands-on adaptations; positions me as a local leader

options.label: Negotiate a hybrid plan with municipal influence
options.consequence: Opens institutional leverage and technical compromises

options.label: Mobilize a public campaign with Arin
options.consequence: Amplifies public pressure and creative direct action

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: I commit to expanding community-led pilots: marsh restoration, neighborhood floodproofing, and shared workshops — scaling local solutions into a replicable model.

With pilot successes, I choose how to scale: formalize a community cooperative, push for municipal adoption of our model, or accept conditional funding from sympathetic private donors. Each option grows capacity differently.

major_decision_point.description: How to institutionalize the grassroots victories.

options.label: Formalize a community cooperative
options.consequence: Local ownership, slower access to capital, strong social bonds

options.label: Push for municipal adoption and policy change
options.consequence: Leverages city resources with institutional legitimacy

options.label: Accept conditional philanthropic funding
options.consequence: Rapid scaling but with donor constraints to navigate

chapter_structure.chapter_title: Cooperative Bloom — Ending (Route A, Ending A1)
chapter_structure.narrative_focus: The community cooperative succeeds: decentralized green infrastructure, new apprenticeships, Noah’s shop becomes a regional hub. Mira and Arin deepen their partnership through shared leadership.
I choose to negotiate a hybrid with municipal influence: I work inside the system with Samir to reshape Aegis proposals into equitable, adaptive policy.

Building on municipal leverage, I choose between three strategies: codify community co-management, demand enforceable relocation protections, or create a city-funded retrofit program. Each path embeds community needs differently.

major_decision_point.description: Which policy instrument to prioritize to institutionalize equity.

options.label: Codify community co-management
options.consequence: Long-term shared governance and local oversight

options.label: Secure enforceable relocation protections
options.consequence: Ensures restitution and home rights for displaced neighbors

options.label: Create city-funded retrofit programs
options.consequence: Funds broad resilience upgrades with municipal accountability

chapter_structure.chapter_title: Co-Management Charter — Ending (Route B, Ending B1)
chapter_structure.narrative_focus: The city adopts a codified community co-management charter. Neighborhoods gain legal authority and funding to steward green infrastructure; Mira’s partnership with municipal actors deepens.
I partner with Arin for a public campaign: photo-documentary actions, rooftop demonstrations, and creative direct interventions that amplify marginalized voices across the city.

The campaign succeeds in drawing attention, and I must choose our next move: institutionalize the movement into a civic coalition, negotiate with Aegis for binding concessions, or scale the campaign regionally with media partnerships.

major_decision_point.description: Where to channel the campaign’s energy for durable gains.

options.label: Institutionalize a civic coalition
options.consequence: Creates a durable organization that can negotiate and deliver programs

options.label: Negotiate binding concessions with Aegis
options.consequence: Leverages public pressure into enforceable corporate commitments

options.label: Scale regionally with media partnerships
options.consequence: Broad awareness and replicable models across other harbors

chapter_structure.chapter_title: Civic Coalition — Ending (Route C, Ending C1)
chapter_structure.narrative_focus: The civic coalition forms, professionalizing grassroots capacity, winning municipal seats and program funding. Mira and Arin become co-directors of a durable movement organization.

Through municipal adoption or targeted policy pushes, a hybrid solution combines Aegis infrastructure with strong relocation protections and community oversight—resulting in a measurable rise in safety without erasing place.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 5, Option B / From Chapter 8, Option B

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: Conditional philanthropic funding and negotiated corporate concessions speed scaling of retrofits and community programs while creating permanent funding lines and regional partnerships.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 5, Option C / From Chapter 11, Option B

chapter_structure.narrative_focus: A city-funded, accountable retrofit program scales resilience across socioeconomic lines; Solace Harbor becomes a model of equitable adaptation, and Mira assumes a new municipal leadership role.
chapter_structure.entry_condition: From Chapter 8, Option C / From Chapter 11, Option C

remarks: World View: Solace Harbor is a near-future, mid-latitude coastal city reshaped by decades of sea-level rise and intensifying storms. Once-distinct neighborhoods now stitch themselves together across stilts, reclaimed rooftops, and raised causeways. Political power centers around a coalition of municipal agencies, emergent community collectives, and multinational resilience firms such as Aegis Corporation. Class and place are tightly bound: the economic core sits behind hardened infrastructure proposals; older dockside districts house multi-generational residents with place-based knowledge. Culture favors pragmatic repair, barter, and adaptive design. Conflict fractures around questions of scale and equity: rapid engineered defenses versus slower, community-led ecological adaptations. Atmosphere is melancholic but not defeated: damp salt air, watercolor fog, and daily rituals of care that promise renewal.
